subject: Math
skill: Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000

lesson.story_mission: Help the Number Trail Rangers repair missing signs on a trail that stretches all the way to 1,000.
lesson.mini_lesson: Numbers to 1,000 can be counted one at a time, read in word form, or skip-counted by equal jumps. Watch carefully at hundreds transitions: 198, 199, 200 and 998, 999, 1,000.
lesson.objective: Count, read, write, and skip-count numbers within 1,000.
lesson.concept_explanation: A sequence follows a rule. Counting by 1 changes to the next number, counting by 5s or 10s makes equal jumps, and counting by 100s changes the hundreds.
lesson.common_misconception: A child may skip over a hundred transition, such as saying 199, 201, because the new hundred sounds different.
lesson.real_world_connection: Counting and reading numbers helps with addresses, pages, game scores, and collecting large groups.
lesson.lesson_summary: Use place value and the counting rule to fill missing numbers and match numerals to word forms.

worked_examples.text: 342 is read as three hundred forty-two.

hint_ladder: Use hundreds, tens, and ones to check the number.
Say the full sequence aloud before choosing.

hints: Notice that this sequence changes by 1 each time; start with 198..
Add 1 to 198 to fill the blank.
Verify 199 by checking that both neighboring jumps follow the 1-step rule.

feedback.correct: Yes. 199 follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

guided_practice.explanation: The sequence counts forward by 1. Adding 1 to 198 gives 199, so the same-size jump is preserved. Checking the next shown number confirms the pattern.

hints: Look at 342 one place at a time: hundreds, tens, then ones.
Name the hundreds first, then read the final two digits as one number.
Verify your wording by matching each part of your answer to the digits in 342.

feedback.correct: Yes. three hundred forty-two follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

guided_practice.explanation: Place value tells how to read the numeral: 342 is three hundred forty-two. The hundreds word matches the hundreds digit, and the remaining words name the tens and ones. Reading the words back gives 342.

hints: Notice that this sequence changes by 5 each time; start with 135..
Add 5 to 135 to fill the blank.
Verify 140 by checking that both neighboring jumps follow the 5-step rule.

feedback.correct: Yes. 140 follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

guided_practice.explanation: The sequence counts forward by 5. Adding 5 to 135 gives 140, so the same-size jump is preserved. Checking the next shown number confirms the pattern.

hints: Notice that this sequence changes by 100 each time; start with 475..
Add 100 to 475 to fill the blank.
Verify 575 by checking that both neighboring jumps follow the 100-step rule.

feedback.correct: Yes. 575 follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

guided_practice.explanation: The sequence counts forward by 100. Adding 100 to 475 gives 575, so the same-size jump is preserved. Checking the next shown number confirms the pattern.

hints: Notice that this sequence changes by 1 each time; start with 999..
Add 1 to 999 to fill the blank.
Verify 1000 by checking that both neighboring jumps follow the 1-step rule.

feedback.correct: Yes. 1000 follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

guided_practice.explanation: The sequence counts forward by 1. Adding 1 to 999 gives 1000, so the same-size jump is preserved. Checking the next shown number confirms the pattern.

hints: Notice that 1,000 has a 1 in the thousands place.
Name the thousands digit first; the three zeros show there are no hundreds, tens, or ones to name.
Verify the words “one thousand” by matching them to the numeral 1,000.

feedback.correct: Yes. one thousand follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

guided_practice.explanation: The numeral 1,000 has 1 in the thousands place and 0 in the hundreds, tens, and ones places, so its word form is one thousand. Writing the words back as a numeral verifies 1,000.

hints: Focus on the number or numbers in this prompt and identify the requested next number.
Use the counting-by-1 rule: neighboring whole numbers differ by exactly 1.
Verify 410 by placing it back into the counting sequence from the prompt.

feedback.correct: Yes. 410 follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

adaptive_question_bank.explanation: Neighboring whole numbers differ by 1. Counting in the direction named in the prompt gives 410; placing it beside the given number confirms there is no skipped number.

hints: Focus on the number or numbers in this prompt and identify the requested previous number.
Use the counting-by-1 rule: neighboring whole numbers differ by exactly 1.
Verify 699 by placing it back into the counting sequence from the prompt.

feedback.correct: Yes. 699 follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

adaptive_question_bank.explanation: Neighboring whole numbers differ by 1. Counting in the direction named in the prompt gives 699; placing it beside the given number confirms there is no skipped number.

adaptive_question_bank.prompt: Which sequence counts forward by 1 across a hundred?
hints: Focus on the gaps in 298, 299, 300.
Compare each neighboring pair and keep only the choice with the same-sized jump.
Verify 298, 299, 300 by checking every jump from left to right.

feedback.correct: Yes. 298, 299, 300 follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

adaptive_question_bank.explanation: The correct choice is 298, 299, 300 because each neighboring number follows the counting rule in the prompt. Checking every jump rules out choices that skip or repeat a number.

misconception_bank.sequence_gap_error: Missing or repeating a number when counting by ones.
misconception_bank.hundreds_transition_error: Losing the sequence when a new hundred or 1,000 appears.
misconception_bank.numeral_word_mismatch: Matching a numeral with a different word form.

review_bank.prompt: A trail sign shows 678, 679, __, 681. Write the missing number.
hints: Notice that this sequence changes by 1 each time; start with 679..
Add 1 to 679 to fill the blank.
Verify 680 by checking that both neighboring jumps follow the 1-step rule.

feedback.correct: Yes. 680 follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

review_bank.explanation: The sequence counts forward by 1. Adding 1 to 679 gives 680, so the same-size jump is preserved. Checking the next shown number confirms the pattern.

question_audio.text: A trail sign shows 678, 679, __, 681. Write the missing number.
question_audio.label: Read Question

review_bank.prompt: The ranger counted 864 pinecones. Write 864 in word form.
hints: In the pinecone total 864, examine the hundreds, tens, and ones places.
Name 8 hundreds first, then read 64 as sixty-four.
Verify your wording by matching eight hundred sixty-four to the digits 8, 6, and 4.

feedback.correct: Yes. eight hundred sixty-four follows the place-value or counting rule.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet. Recheck the hundreds, tens, and ones or compare the size of each counting jump.

review_bank.explanation: The numeral 864 has 8 hundreds, 6 tens, and 4 ones, so its word form is eight hundred sixty-four. Matching each word to its digit verifies the numeral 864.

question_audio.text: The ranger counted 864 pinecones. Write 864 in word form.
question_audio.label: Read Question

game_theme.mission_intro: Repair the trail signs by counting and reading numbers to 1,000.

story.text: Listen to this page. You are starting a math mission about Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000. Look at the story and think about what the characters need to solve. Math missions are easier when you name the numbers and the model first. Press Start Mission when you are ready.
story.label: Read This Page

lesson.text: This lesson teaches Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000. Look at the number sequence and connect each part to the math words. Use the vocabulary on the page to explain your thinking. Remember to check place value, units, or symbols before you answer. Press Next to continue.
lesson.label: Read This Page

watch.text: This page lets you watch a worked example for Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000. Follow the model one step at a time. Say what each number means before you look at the final answer. If a symbol appears, read it in words like plus, minus, equals, greater than, or less than. Press Next to continue.
watch.label: Read This Page

demo.text: This demo helps you try Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000 with support. Build or study the model first, then connect it to the question. A good strategy is to explain the answer in math words before choosing. Press Next when you are ready for practice.
demo.label: Read This Page

practice.text: This is practice. Use the visual model to help you think about Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000. Press Read Question if you want the exact question read aloud. Check your strategy before you choose or write an answer. When you are ready, answer the question and keep going.
practice.label: Read This Page

challenge.text: This is a challenge. Use the visual model to help you think about Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000. Press Read Question if you want the exact question read aloud. Check your strategy before you choose or write an answer. When you are ready, answer the question and keep going.
challenge.label: Read This Page

checkpoint.text: This is your checkpoint. Use the visual model to help you think about Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000. Press Read Question if you want the exact question read aloud. Check your strategy before you choose or write an answer. When you are ready, answer the question and keep going.
checkpoint.label: Read This Page

badge.text: This badge page celebrates your mission work on Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000. Look at your stars and remember the strategy you used. Mistakes help show what to practice next. Save your growth data to continue.
badge.label: Read This Page

profile.text: This profile page shows your growth for Count, Read, and Write Numbers to 1,000. Look at attempts, correct answers, accuracy, and hints used. Use the recommendation to decide what to practice next. You can continue to Skill Practice or replay the mission.
profile.label: Read This Page
